revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:هاكو إيتشو]][[bg:Хако ичиу]][[en:Hakkō ichiu]][[es:Hakkō ichiu]][[fa:هاکو ایچیو]][[fr:Hakkō ichiu]][[he:האקו איצ'יו]][[id:Hakkō ichiu]][[it:Hakkō ichiu]][[ja:八紘一宇]][[ko:팔굉일우]][[pt:Hakkō ichiu]][[ru:Восемь углов мира под одной крышей]][[uk:Весь світ під одним дахом]][[vi:Hakkō ichiu]][[zh:八紘一宇]]
{{Short description|Japanese imperialist slogan popular during World War II}} {{Italic title}} {{Use mdy dates|date=September 2011}} {{Infobox Chinese | kanji = 八紘一宇 | kana = はっこういちう | revhep = Hakkō ichiu }} {{Infobox Chinese | kyujitai = 八紘爲宇 | shinjitai = 八紘為宇 | kana = はっこういう | revhep = Hakkō iu | title = Hakkō iu }}
[[File:HakkoIchiu10sen.jpg|thumb|300px|10-sen Japanese banknote, illustrating the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} monument in [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki]], first issued in 1944]]
{{Nihongo3|"eight crown cords, one roof", i.e. "all the world under one roof"|八紘一宇|'''Hakkō ichiu'''}} or {{transliteration|ja|'''hakkō iu'''}} ({{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shinjitai|en|Shinjitai|qid=Q1055887|lt=Shinjitai|1=Shinjitai}}}}: {{lang|ja|八紘為宇}}, {{lang|ja|八紘爲宇}}) was a Japanese {{ill|political slogan|en|political slogan|qid=Q2101619|lt=political slogan|1=Political slogan}} meaning the divine right of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} to "{{ill|World domination|en|World domination|qid=Q540229|lt=unify the eight corners of the world|1=World domination}}." The slogan formed the basis of the empire's ideology. It was prominent from the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q170314|lt=Second Sino-Japanese War|1=Second Sino-Japanese War}} to {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} and was popularized in a speech by {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|qid=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}} on January 8, 1940.<ref>Beasley, Japanese Imperialism 1894–1945, pp. 226–7.</ref>
==Background== [[File:Benkan emperor komei.jpg|thumb|right|The {{transliteration|ja|[[mianguan#Benkan (Japan)|benkan]]}} of [[:en:Emperor Komei|Emperor Komei]]]] The term was coined early in the 20th century by the {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|qid=Q871371|lt=Nichiren|1=Nichiren Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} activist and nationalist {{ill|Tanaka Chigaku|en|Tanaka Chigaku|qid=Q7682247|lt=Tanaka Chigaku|1=Tanaka Chigaku}}, who cobbled it from parts of a statement attributed in the chronicle {{transliteration|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} to legendary first {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} at the time of his ascension.{{efn|As early as 1928, the Japanese editorials were already preaching the theme of the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} without using the specific term.<ref>Michio Nakajima, ''Tennō no daigawari to kokumin'', Aoki Shoten 1990, pp. 129–30.</ref>}} The emperor's full statement reads: {{nihongo||八紘を掩うて宇と為さん|"Hakkō wo ooute ie to nasan"}} (in the original {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kanbun|en|Kanbun|qid=Q7901319|lt=Kanbun|1=Kanbun}}}}: {{lang|ja|掩八紘而爲宇}}), and means: "I shall cover the eight directions and make them my abode". The term {{nihongo||八紘|hakkō}}, meaning "eight crown cords" ("crown cords" being the hanging decorations of the {{nihongo||冕冠|[[mianguan#Benkan (Japan)|benkan]]}}, a traditional Chinese-style crown), was a metaphor for {{nihongo||八方|happō}}, or "eight directions".<ref>Jitō 字統, Shirakawa Shizuka, Heibonsha, 1994, p. 302, 紘 entry.</ref>{{efn|The {{transliteration|ja|kun'yomi}}-reading {{transliteration|ja|ie}} for the {{transliteration|ja|on'yomi}}-reading {{nihongo||宇|u}} is now defunct, but at the time of the {{transliteration|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, readings were not yet fixed in the way that was later to become the case. Rather, any meaning associated with a Chinese character as used in Chinese was in theory available as a reading, as evidenced by the sometimes-extreme variation in the writing of even common words in the {{transliteration|ja|Nihon Shoki}}.}}
Despite its original universalist meaning, according to the principle of "ichi soku issai, issai soku ichi (one is inseparable from the whole and vice versa)", Tanaka interpreted it as justification for imperialism. To stop this imperialist reinterpretation from spreading, Koyama Iwao (1905–1993), disciple of [[:en:Kitaro Nishida|Nishida]], and drawing off the ''{{ill|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|en|Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra|qid=Q790258|lt=Flower Adornment Sutra|1=Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra}}'', proposed to substitute the words "to be included or to find a place" for the last two characters ("to make them my abode"). That move was rejected by the military circles of the nationalist right.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Jonckheere|first1=Fabrice|chapter=Hakko ichiu theory (八 纮 一 宇)|chapter-url=http://jonckheeref.com/html/jonckheere_hakko_ichiu_eng.html|title=Pacific War: the initial expansion|series=History of the last war no. 15|publisher=Caraktere ed|year=2011|issn=2104-807X|page=62|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131111112821/http://jonckheeref.com/html/jonckheere_hakko_ichiu_eng.html|archive-date=November 11, 2013|df=mdy-all}}</ref><ref>See also Kosei Ishii</ref>
==Origins== {{See|Racial Equality Proposal, 1919|Jewish settlement in Imperial Japan}} [[File:Founding Ceremony of the Hakko-Ichiu Monument.JPG|thumb|upright|Founding ceremony of the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} monument on April 3, 1940. It had [[:en:Prince Chichibu|Prince Chichibu]]'s calligraphy of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} carved on its front side.<ref>David C. Earhart, ''Certain Victory'', 2008, p. 63.</ref>]] [[File:2600th year of Japanese Imperial Calendar stamp of 10sen.jpg|thumb|upright|Prewar 10-sen Japanese stamp, illustrating the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} and the 2,600th anniversary of the Empire]] [[File:Celebrating Gagaku Music in Imperial 2600.JPG|thumb|upright|[[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]] and {{ill|Empress Nagako|en|Empress Nagako|qid=Q230697|lt=Empress Nagako|1=Empress Nagako}} preside the celebration of the 2,600th anniversary of mythical foundation of the empire in November 1940.]] [[File:Flag of Hakkoichiu.jpg|thumb|upright|Japanese pilots who gathered under the flag of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} during the {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}]] There were enough Japanese in Western nations that suffered from {{ill|racial discrimination|en|racial discrimination|qid=Q12332701|lt=racial discrimination|1=Racial discrimination}} issues that in 1919, Japan proposed a [[:en:Racial Equality Proposal, 1919|racial equality clause]] at the [[:en:Paris Peace Conference, 1919|Paris Peace Conference]]. The proposal, intended to only apply to {{ill|League of Nations|en|League of Nations|qid=Q38130|lt=League of Nations|1=League of Nations}} members,{{sfn|Shimazu|1998|p=114}} received the support of a majority but was vetoed by US President {{ill|Woodrow Wilson|en|Woodrow Wilson|qid=Q34296|lt=Woodrow Wilson|1=Woodrow Wilson}} in violation of the rules of the Conference that allowed a majority vote. In 1924, the [[:en:US Congress|US Congress]] enacted the [[:en:Asian Exclusion Act|Asian Exclusion Act]], outlawing immigration from Asia.
Worsened with the economic impact of the {{ill|Shōwa financial crisis|en|Shōwa financial crisis|qid=Q3002770|lt=Shōwa financial crisis|1=Shōwa financial crisis}} and the {{ill|Great Depression|en|Great Depression|qid=Q8698|lt=Great Depression|1=Great Depression}} in the 1930s, which led to a resurgence of nationalist, militarist and expansionist movement, [[:en:Emperor Shōwa|Emperor Shōwa]], known more commonly as Hirohito outside Japan, and his reign became associated with the rediscovery of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} as an expansionist element of Japanese nationalistic beliefs.<ref>Bix, Herbert. (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=zjmVltzm1kYC&q=Hirohito+and+the+Making+of+Modern+Japan ''Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan,'' p. 201.]</ref> The naval limitations treaties of 1921 and especially 1930 were seen as a mistake{{Clarify|date=August 2010}} in their unanticipated effect on internal political struggles in Japan, and the treaties provided an external motivating catalyst that provoked reactionary militarist elements to desperate actions, with their presence overtaking civilian and liberal elements in society.<ref>Morison, Samuel Eliot. (1948). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BozSWv25Gd8C&dq=Hakko+Ichiu:&pg=PA3 ''History of United States Naval Operations in World War II: The Battle of the Atlantic, September 1939 – May 1943,'' pp. 3–10.]</ref>
The evolution of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} serves as a changing litmus test of those factional relationships during the next decade.<ref>John Pike, (2011). [http://www.globalsecurity.org/military/world/japan/kodo.htm "Kodo (Way of the Emperor)"]. ''GlobalSecurity.org''</ref>
The term {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} did not enter general circulation until 1940, when the second Konoe administration issued a {{ill|white paper|en|white paper|qid=Q223729|lt=white paper|1=White paper}} titled {{nihongo|"Fundamental National Policy"|[[:ja:基本国策要綱|基本国策要綱]]|Kihon Kokusaku Yōkō}}, which opened with those words and in which Prime Minister Konoe proclaimed that the basic aim of Japan's national policy was "the establishment of world peace in conformity with the very spirit in which our nation was founded."<ref>Edwards, p. 309.</ref>{{efn|In the original text, {{lang|ja|「肇国の大精神に基き世界平和の確立を招来すること」}}}} and that the first step was the proclamation of a {{nihongo|"new order in East Asia"|[[:ja:東亜新秩序|東亜新秩序]]|Tōa Shin Chitsujo}}, which later took the form of the "{{ill|Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere|en|Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere|qid=Q696192|lt=Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere|1=Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere}}".<ref>James L. McClain, ''Japan: A Modern History'' p 470 {{ISBN|0-393-04156-5}}</ref> In the most magnanimous form, the term was used to indicate the making of a universal brotherhood implemented by the uniquely-virtuous {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|qid=Q640605|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato people}}.<ref>{{ill|Piers Brendon|en|Piers Brendon|qid=Q15458673|lt=Piers Brendon|1=Piers Brendon}}, ''The Dark Valley: A Panorama of the 1930s'', p43 {{ISBN|0-375-40881-9}}</ref> Because that would bring people under the emperor's fatherly benevolence, force was justified against those who resisted.<ref>{{ill|Herbert P. Bix|en|Herbert P. Bix|qid=Q4202056|lt=Herbert P. Bix|1=Herbert P. Bix}}, ''{{ill|Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan|en|Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan|qid=Q5770206|lt=Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan|1=Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan}}'' p 11 {{ISBN|0-06-019314-X}}</ref>
The Japanese additionally undertook many projects to prove that they supported racial equality. For example, on December 6, 1938, the Five Ministers Council (Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|qid=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}}, [[:en:Ministry of War of Japan|Army Minister]] {{ill|Seishirō Itagaki|en|Seishirō Itagaki|qid=Q704374|lt=Seishirō Itagaki|1=Seishirō Itagaki}}, [[:en:Ministry of the Navy of Japan|Navy Minister]] {{ill|Mitsumasa Yonai|en|Mitsumasa Yonai|qid=Q715231|lt=Mitsumasa Yonai|1=Mitsumasa Yonai}}, [[:en:Foreign Minister|Foreign Minister]] {{ill|Hachirō Arita|en|Hachirō Arita|qid=Q668406|lt=Hachirō Arita|1=Hachirō Arita}} and [[:en:Finance Minister|Finance Minister]] [[:en:Shigeaki Ikeda|Shigeaki Ikeda]]), the highest decision-making council at the time,<ref name="mof">{{cite web|title=Question 戦前の日本における対ユダヤ人政策の基本をなしたと言われる「ユダヤ人対策要綱」に関する史料はありますか。また、同要綱に関する説明文はありますか。|url=http://www.mofa.go.jp/mofaj/annai/honsho/shiryo/qa/senzen_03.html|access-date=2010-09-21|publisher=[[Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan]]}}</ref><ref name="gosho">{{cite web|date=1938-12-06|title=猶太人対策要綱|url=http://www.jacar.go.jp/DAS/meta/listPhoto?IS_STYLE=default&ID=M2006092115064531921|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110726042931/http://www.jacar.go.jp/DAS/meta/listPhoto?IS_STYLE=default&ID=M2006092115064531921|archive-date=July 26, 2011|access-date=2010-09-21|work=Five ministers council|publisher=[[National Archives of Japan|Japan Center for Asian Historical Record]]|page=36/42|df=mdy-all}}</ref> took the decision to prohibit the expulsion of the {{ill|Jews|en|Jews|qid=Q7325|lt=Jews|1=Jews}} from Japan, Manchuria, and {{ill|Republic of China (1912–1949)|en|Republic of China (1912–1949)|qid=Q13426199|lt=China|1=Republic of China (1912–1949)}}.<ref name="mof" /><ref name="gosho" /> Thereafter, the Japanese received [[:en:Jewish refugees|Jewish refugees]] despite the opposition of their ally {{ill|Nazi Germany|en|Nazi Germany|qid=Q7318|lt=Nazi Germany|1=Nazi Germany}}.
1940 was declared the 2,600th anniversary of the founding of Japan in part to celebrate {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}}.<ref>[[:en:Edwin P. Hoyt|Edwin P. Hoyt]], ''Japan's War'', p 196 {{ISBN|0-07-030612-5}}</ref> As part of the celebrations, the government officially opened the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} monument (now Heiwadai Tower) at what is now {{ill|Heiwadai Park|en|Heiwadai Park|qid=Q3364135|lt=Miyazaki Peace Park|1=Heiwadai Park}} in the city of [[:en:Miyazaki, Miyazaki|Miyazaki]].
==World War II== As the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q170314|lt=Second Sino-Japanese War|1=Second Sino-Japanese War}} dragged on without conclusion, the Japanese government turned increasingly to the nation's spiritual capital to maintain fighting spirit.
Characterization of the fighting as a {{Nihongo|"holy war"|聖戦|seisen}}, similarly grounding the current conflict in the nation's sacred beginnings, became increasingly evident in the Japanese press at this time. In 1940, the {{ill|Imperial Rule Assistance Association|en|Imperial Rule Assistance Association|qid=Q714797|lt=Imperial Rule Assistance Association|1=Imperial Rule Assistance Association}} was launched to provide political support to Japan's war in China.
The general spread of the term {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}}, neatly encapsulating this view of expansion as mandated in Japan's divine origin, was further propelled by preparations for [[:ja:紀元二千六百年記念行事|celebrating the 2,600th anniversary of Jimmu's ascension]], which fell in the year 1940 according to the traditional chronology. Stories recounted that Jimmu, finding five races in Japan, had made them all as "brothers of one family".<ref>{{ill|John W. Dower|en|John W. Dower|qid=Q903632|lt=John W. Dower|1=John W. Dower}}, ''War Without Mercy: Race & Power in the Pacific War'' p223 {{ISBN|0-394-50030-X}}</ref>
===Propaganda purposes=== After Japan declared war on the Allies in December 1941, Allied governments produced several {{ill|Propaganda film|en|Propaganda film|qid=Q1935609|lt=Propaganda film|1=Propaganda film}}s citing the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} as evidence that the Japanese intended to {{ill|World domination|en|World domination|qid=Q540229|lt=conquer the entire world|1=World domination}}.
To win the support of the conquered, [[:en:Japanese propaganda during World War II|Japanese propaganda]] included phrases such as "Asia for the Asians!" and emphasized about the perceived need to liberate Asian countries from imperialist powers.<ref name="rhodes248">Anthony Rhodes, ''Propaganda: The Art of Persuasion: World War II'', p248 1976, Chelsea House Publishers, New York</ref> Japan's failure to win the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|qid=Q170314|lt=war in China|1=Second Sino-Japanese War}} was blamed on European nations and the US exploiting their Asian colonies to assist Chinese forces, even though the Chinese received far more assistance from the Soviet Union.<ref>James L. McClain, ''Japan: A Modern History'' p 471 {{ISBN|0-393-04156-5}}</ref> In some cases local populations welcomed Japanese troops when they invaded, initially seeing them as preferable to being ruled by Western colonial powers.<ref name="rhodes248" /> The Japanese also indoctrinated their soldiers into believing that it was their duty to make Asians "strong again" through force, after being weakened by Western imperialism.<ref name="John W. Dower p24-5">John W. Dower, ''War Without Mercy: Race & Power in the Pacific War'' p24-5 {{ISBN|0-394-50030-X}}</ref>
The official translation offered by contemporary leaders was "universal brotherhood", but it was widely acknowledged that that expression meant that the Japanese were "equal to the Caucasians but, to the peoples of Asia, we act as their leader".<ref>Stephen S. Large. ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=JD1iyqWwHQcC&pg=PA202 Shōwa Japan]''. New York: Taylor & Francis, 1998. p. 202.</ref> Hence {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} could be seen as a euphemism for Japanese supremacy. In fact, the brutality and the racism of the Japanese led the conquered to view the Japanese imperialists as being equal to or sometimes worse than Western imperialists.<ref name="rhodes248"/> For example, the economies of most occupied territories were remanaged only to produce raw war materials for Japan.<ref>James L. McClain, ''Japan: A Modern History'' p 495 {{ISBN|0-393-04156-5}}</ref>
===Allied judgment=== {{transliteration|ja|Hakkō ichiu}} meant the bringing together of the corners of the world under one ruler, or the making of the world's one family.<ref name=judgment/> That was the alleged ideal of the foundation of the empire, and, in its traditional context, meant no more than a universal principle of {{ill|Humanity (virtue)|en|Humanity (virtue)|qid=Q12767209|lt=humanity|1=Humanity (virtue)}}, which was destined ultimately to pervade the whole universe.<ref name=judgment/> The way to the realisation of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} was through the benign rule of the Emperor, and therefore the "way of the Emperor," the "Imperial" or the "Kingly way," was a concept of virtue and a maxim of conduct.<ref name=judgment/> {{transliteration|ja|Hakkō ichiu}} was the moral goal, and loyalty to the Emperor was the road that led to it.<ref name=judgment/> Throughout the years that followed measures of military aggression were advocated in the names of {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}}, which eventually became symbols for world domination through military force.<ref name=judgment>{{cite web|last=Clancey |first=Patrick |url=http://www.ibiblio.org/hyperwar/PTO/IMTFE/IMTFE-4.html|title= Chapter IV: The Military Domination of Japan and Preparations for War |website=HyperWar: International Military Tribunal for the Far East}}</ref>
==Aftermath== [[File:Heiwadai tower.jpg|thumb|left|{{transliteration|ja|Hakkō ichiu}} monument (renamed Tower of Peace) in 2010]] Since the end of the Pacific War, some have highlighted the {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} slogan as part of a context of {{ill|Historical revisionism|en|Historical revisionism|qid=Q874803|lt=Historical revisionism|1=Historical revisionism}}.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Goodman|first=David G.|title="Visas and Virtue by Chris Tashima" [review]|journal=Journal of Japanese Studies|volume=26|issue=1 (Winter, 2000)|pages=266–269|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/133422|doi=10.2307/133422|jstor=133422 |quote=Japanese historical revisionists, however, assert that Sugihara was not in fact a renegade but an exemplary diplomat who... dutifully carrying out the high-minded Japanese policy of racial harmony under the aegis of the emperor (''hakko ichiu'')}}</ref> The {{transliteration|ja|hakkō ichiu}} monument was renamed {{nihongo3|Tower of Peace|平和の塔|Heiwa no tou}} in 1958 and still stands today. The writing "{{transliteration|ja|Hakkō ichiu}}" was removed from it after the Japanese defeat at the insistence of the U.S. military.<ref name=times>{{cite news |last=Motomura |first=Hiroshi |date=February 10, 2015 |title=Miyazaki's controversial Peace Tower continues to cause unease |url=https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2015/02/10/national/miyazakis-controversial-peace-tower-continues-to-cause-unease/#.WrKBvOjwZph |work=Japan Times |access-date=March 21, 2018}}</ref> The tower was the inception point for the torch relay of the {{ill|1964 Summer Olympics|en|1964 Summer Olympics|qid=Q8420|lt=1964 Summer Olympics|1=1964 Summer Olympics}}.<ref name=times /> After the Olympics, which coincided with worldwide interest in the Japanese Imperial family, the local tourism association successfully petitioned the {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} to reinstall the "{{transliteration|ja|Hakkō ichiu}}" characters.<ref name=times /> {{Clear}}
==See also== * ''{{ill|An Investigation of Global Policy with the Yamato Race as Nucleus|en|An Investigation of Global Policy with the Yamato Race as Nucleus|qid=Q4750110|lt=An Investigation of Global Policy with the Yamato Race as Nucleus|1=An Investigation of Global Policy with the Yamato Race as Nucleus}}'' * {{ill|Greater East Asia Conference|en|Greater East Asia Conference|qid=Q702431|lt=Greater East Asia Conference|1=Greater East Asia Conference}} * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Hokushin-ron|en|Hokushin-ron|qid=Q20982946|lt=Hokushin-ron|1=Hokushin-ron}}}} * {{ill|Japanese militarism|en|Japanese militarism|qid=Q702868|lt=Japanese militarism|1=Japanese militarism}} * {{ill|Japanese nationalism|en|Japanese nationalism|qid=Q1331440|lt=Japanese nationalism|1=Japanese nationalism}} * [[:en:Statism in Shōwa Japan|Statism in Shōwa Japan]] * {{lang|de|{{ill|Lebensraum|en|Lebensraum|qid=Q157136|lt=Lebensraum|1=Lebensraum}}}} * {{ill|Manifest destiny|en|Manifest destiny|qid=Q474184|lt=Manifest destiny|1=Manifest destiny}} * {{ill|Moscow, third Rome|en|Moscow, third Rome|qid=Q65167706|lt=Moscow, third Rome|1=Moscow, third Rome}} * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Nanshin-ron|en|Nanshin-ron|qid=Q6150938|lt=Nanshin-ron|1=Nanshin-ron}}}} * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shinmin no Michi|en|Shinmin no Michi|qid=Q7497669|lt=Shinmin no Michi|1=Shinmin no Michi}}}} * {{lang|it|{{ill|Spazio vitale|en|Spazio vitale|qid=Q7574161|lt=Spazio vitale|1=Spazio vitale}}}} * {{ill|Tanaka Memorial|en|Tanaka Memorial|qid=Q717249|lt=Tanaka Memorial|1=Tanaka Memorial}} * {{ill|World domination|en|World domination|qid=Q540229|lt=World domination|1=World domination}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist|2}}
==Bibliography== * {{ill|William G. Beasley|en|William G. Beasley|qid=Q3181262|lt=Beasley, William G.|1=William G. Beasley}} (1991). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SScFJQAACAAJ&q=Japanese+Imperialism+1894-1945 ''Japanese Imperialism 1894–1945.''] Oxford: Oxford University. {{ISBN|978-0-19-822168-5}}. * {{ill|Herbert P. Bix|en|Herbert P. Bix|qid=Q4202056|lt=Bix, Herbert P.|1=Herbert P. Bix}} (2001). [https://books.google.com/books?id=zjmVltzm1kYC&q=Hirohito+and+the+Making+of+Modern+Japan ''Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan.''] New York: {{ill|HarperCollins|en|HarperCollins|qid=Q95149|lt=HarperCollins|1=HarperCollins}}. {{ISBN|978-0-06-093130-8}}. * Brendon, Piers (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=3dUMAAAACAAJ&q=The+Dark+Valley:+A+Panorama+of+the+1930s ''The Dark Valley: A Panorama of the 1930s.''] New York: {{ill|Vintage Books|en|Vintage Books|qid=Q3560313|lt=Vintage|1=Vintage Books}}. {{ISBN|978-0-375-70808-4}}. * Brownlee, John (1997). ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=eatISvupicUC&q=brownlee+Japan Japanese Historians and the National Myths, 1600–1945: The Age of the Gods.]'' Vancouver: {{ill|University of British Columbia Press|en|University of British Columbia Press|qid=Q7895143|lt=University of British Columbia Press|1=University of British Columbia Press}}. {{ISBN|0-7748-0645-1}}. * Earhart, David C. (2007). [https://books.google.com/books?id=WffIAAAACAAJ&q=Certain+Victory+Earhart ''Certain Victory: Images of World War II in the Japanese Media.''] Armonk, New York: M.E. Sharpe. {{ISBN|978-0-7656-1776-7}}. * Edwards, Walter. [https://web.archive.org/web/20081226110254/http://depts.washington.edu/jjs/edwards2.htm "Forging Tradition for a Holy War: The ''Hakkō Ichiu'' Tower in Miyazaki and Japanese Wartime Ideology."] ''Journal of Japanese Studies'' 29:2 (2003). * {{ill|Samuel Eliot Morison|en|Samuel Eliot Morison|qid=Q982883|lt=Morison, Samuel Eliot|1=Samuel Eliot Morison}} (1948). [https://books.google.com/books?id=BozSWv25Gd8C&q=Hakko+Ichiu: ''History of United States Naval Operations in World War II: The Battle of the Atlantic, September 1939 – May 1943.''] Oxford: {{ill|Oxford University Press|en|Oxford University Press|qid=Q217595|lt=Oxford University Press|1=Oxford University Press}}. 40 editions – [reprinted by {{ill|University of Illinois Press|en|University of Illinois Press|qid=Q2080232|lt=University of Illinois Press|1=University of Illinois Press}} at Urbana, 2001. {{ISBN|978-0-252-06973-4}}]. * Kosei, Ishii (2002). [http://www.inalco.fr/ina_gabarit_rubrique.php3?id_rubrique=1882&pub=1&id_publication=308 ''The idea of "co-prosperity Sphere of Greater East Asia" and the Buddhist philosophy - the role of the School of Kyoto.''] Paris: [[:en:Inalco|Inalco]]. {{ISBN|2-85831-105-6}}. * {{cite book |last=Shimazu |first=Naoko |title=Japan, Race and Equality |year=1998 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=0-415-17207-1}}
==External links== {{Wiktionary pipe|八紘一宇|hakkō ichiu}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20131111112821/http://jonckheeref.com/html/jonckheere_hakko_ichiu_eng.html "Hakkō ichiu theory ({{nihongo2|八紘一宇}})", in "All-Out Japanese Attacks"] [http://www.derniere-guerre.com/derniere-guerre-15.php ''History of the Last War'' no. 15 (2011)].
